help.title: Help — {context}
help.home.welcome.heading: What is Cadence?
help.home.welcome.1: Cadence is a spaced repetition tool — it helps you learn and retain anything over time by showing you what to review at exactly the right moment.
help.home.welcome.2: Create cards for what you want to learn, group them in decks, and review regularly. Cadence handles the scheduling.
help.home.welcome.3: The more consistently you review, the better the algorithm models your memory — and the less time you need to maintain what you know.
help.home.nav.heading: Why spaced repetition works
help.home.nav.1: Reviewing something just before you're likely to forget it strengthens the memory far more than reviewing it while it's still fresh.
help.home.nav.2: Short, consistent sessions beat long, infrequent ones — 10 minutes a day outperforms an hour once a week.
help.home.nav.3: Well-reviewed cards are shown less and less often — knowledge gradually moves into long-term memory.
help.folder.what.heading: What is a folder?
help.folder.what.1: A folder groups related decks together. It's purely organisational and doesn't affect the learning algorithm.
help.folder.what.2: Folders can be nested inside other folders to build any hierarchy that suits your needs.
help.folder.what.3: Click the folder icon in the sidebar to expand or collapse it. Drag folders and decks to reorganise them.
help.library.browse.heading: What is the library?
help.library.browse.1: The library is the master list of all your cards, across all decks. A card can belong to multiple decks simultaneously.
help.library.browse.2: Each card shows two dots: recall score (red/yellow/green) and ease. These reflect your review history.
help.library.browse.3: Use the library to search, filter by tag or deck, and manage cards independently of any particular deck.
help.library.browse.4: Cards deleted here are permanently removed from all decks. Removing a card from a deck (via the deck view) only unlinks it.
help.deck.study.heading: What is a deck?
help.deck.study.1: A deck is a collection of cards you study together.
help.deck.study.2: A card can belong to multiple decks simultaneously — organise the same material in different ways without duplicating anything.
help.deck.study.3: When you study a deck, only cards whose recall score has dropped below your threshold are shown.
help.deck.manage.heading: Study strategies & importance
help.deck.manage.1: The three study strategies differ in how they prioritise cards. Each is described in the strategy picker on the deck page.
help.deck.manage.2: Importance is a weight that determines how often a card is prioritised relative to others in Optimal and Stochastic modes.
help.deck.manage.3: Each card has a base importance. Inside a deck, you can override this with a deck-specific value that only affects that deck.
help.deck.bar.heading: Deck metrics
help.deck.bar.1: Recall: average probability of recalling a card right now. Drops over time without review.
help.deck.bar.2: Stability: how long memories last before dropping below threshold. Grows with each successful review.
help.deck.bar.3: Ease: how naturally cards are recalled on average. Reflects difficulty history.
help.deck.bar.4: Mastery (X/Y): cards currently above the recall threshold out of the total.
help.card.edit.heading: What is a card?
help.card.edit.1: A card represents one thing you want to learn — a tune, a concept, a word, anything with a name.
help.card.edit.2: Each card has tags (to categorise it), an importance score (to prioritise it), and optional attachments: notes, audio files, ABC notation, links.
help.card.edit.3: Cadence tracks your recall history for each card individually and uses the FSRS algorithm to decide when to show it next.
help.card.edit.4: A card is independent of any deck — it can be linked to multiple decks and removed from one without affecting the others.
help.card.fsrs.heading: Memory metrics
help.card.fsrs.1: Recall: the probability you can recall this card right now. Decays over time, resets upward after each review.
help.card.fsrs.2: Stability: how long the memory lasts. Grows with each successful recall — especially with Easy ratings.
help.card.fsrs.3: Ease: how naturally the card comes to you. Built from your difficulty history — improves as the card becomes more familiar.
help.card.abc.heading: ABC files
help.card.abc.1: Click an .abc attachment to open the viewer with playback controls.
help.card.abc.2: If the file contains multiple versions, use ← → in the player toolbar to navigate between them.
help.card.history.heading: Review history
help.card.history.1: Click "+" in the review history to manually log a past session.
help.card.history.2: Hover an entry and click ✕ to remove it.
help.study.rating.heading: How studying works
help.study.rating.1: Cadence shows you cards whose recall score has dropped below your threshold — the ones you're most likely to have forgotten.
help.study.rating.2: After recalling a card, rate how easily it came to you. This is the key input the algorithm uses to schedule the next review.
help.study.rating.3: The harder it was to recall, the sooner the card will come back. The easier, the longer before you see it again.
help.study.rating.4: Be honest with your ratings — the algorithm works best when they reflect your actual recall.
help.study.shortcuts.heading: Rating scale
help.study.shortcuts.1: ✗ Again — no recall. The card resets and will reappear soon.
help.study.shortcuts.2: △ Hard — recalled with significant effort. Interval grows slowly.
help.study.shortcuts.3: ○ Good — correct recall with reasonable effort. Standard progression.
help.study.shortcuts.4: ✓ Easy — instant, effortless recall. Longest interval increase.
help.study.fsrs.heading: Tips
help.study.fsrs.1: You don't need to review everything — Cadence will only show cards that need it. Trust the scheduler.
help.study.fsrs.2: Consistency matters more than session length. A few cards every day beats a large batch once a week.
help.study.fsrs.3: Cards above your recall threshold are skipped automatically — they don't need reviewing yet.
